alternatives.text: Just the application (one import at a time)
The application and the connector (makes importing easy and mass imports fast)

branchingQuestion.question: There are two ways to use the Zotero application. You can either just use the application by itself, or you can use the application and the connector. How do you want to explore Zotero?

params.text: This is your Zotero Library! It' emply right now but let's fix that.
Click on this folder icon and type in any name you want and select "Create Collection". This will create a new folder for you to start importing into.

metadata.title: This is your Zotero Library! It' emply right now but let's fix that. Click on this folder icon and type in any name you want and select "Create Collection". This will create a new folder for you to start importing into.
Click the blue "Download" button and wait for the application to download onto your computer. Once downloaded, click on the download to open the application.
Click the blue "Download" button and wait for the application to download onto your computer. Once downloaded, click on the download and open the application.

params.text: Click on their citation function.

metadata.title: Find an a resource you want to use. For this example, I am using an article from ProQuest One Business on Business Management, but this will work on any database with citation features. Click on their citation function.

params.text: You are looking for a citation export function in RIS format. This is where it is on ProQuest.

metadata.title: Find the citation export function and download the file in RIS format
You will get a pop-up with some settings. Just make sure you are downloading in RIS format and hit "Continue" and the item will save to your computer.

params.text: Go back to your Zotero application and click on "File".
Next, click on "Import"

metadata.title: Next, we're going to upload into the Zotero application. Go back to your Zotero application and click on "File". Next, click on "Import".
Select your downloaded RIS file to upload into Zotero. Click on "open".
Now, the item's record is in your application! Yay! Next, let's get the PDF uploaded to it so that you can read it in the application.

params.text: Here's the "Download PDF" button! Let's click on it and save the PDF to our computer. Next, we're back in the Zotero app.

metadata.title: Let's go back to ProQuest and find the "Download PDF" button. Click on it and download the item.

params.text: Click on the item to highlight it in blue and have the column on the right appear.
Next we're going to scroll down the column until we reach a section titled "Attachments"
Click on the "+"
Click on "File"

metadata.title: Okay, we're back in Zotero. Click on the item to highlight it in blue and on the right side you should have a column appear. In the column, scroll to the "Attachments" section and select the "+". Then select "File".
Select the downloaded PDF and hit "Open"
Double click on the resource and congradulations!! Now you can start reading, highlightling and mapping your research in Zotero!

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Basics - Downloading the app
feedback.subtitle: What else would you like to explore?

metadata.title: Go back to the Zotero downloads page and click on the second blue "Download" button. This should say: "Install [web browser] Connector".

Go back to your browser. (I am using Chrome). Click on your extensions (The puzzle piece next to the star on Chrome) and you should see Zotero. Click on the pin icon to "pin" it to your extensions bar. This looks slightly different on each web browser.
Now, you should have a little Z, paper or manilla folder icon next to the puzzle piece

params.text: This is your Zotero Library! It' emply right now but let's fix that.
Click on this folder icon and type in any name you want and select "Create Collection". This will create a new folder for you to start importing into.

metadata.title: This is your Zotero Library! It' emply right now but let's fix that. Click on this folder icon and type in any name you want and select "Create Collection". This will create a new folder for you to start importing into.

params.text: For the sake of this example, let's say we're interested in reading all the results on this first page.
Click on the manilla folder we just added to our extensions bar

metadata.title: Now, let's find some resources we want! I'm going to use ProQuest One Business but this works on any database. We're going to do a mass import.

params.text: You can select all the articles, or pick and choose a few.
Hit "Okay" once you are done choosing your articles

This dialog box shows you which folder these items are saving to. Your's should say "Saving to: [Your folder name]" If it does not, or if you want it to save somewhere different, you can click the down arrow to see more places these articles can be saved within Zotero.

metadata.title: Now, two dialog boxes should open. You can select all the articles, or pick and choose a few. One dialog box shows you which folder these items are saving to. Your's should say "Saving to: [Your folder]". Hit "Okay"

Items will start downloading and will be reflected in the dialog box. Do not click anything while these items are downloading as it may stop the download process. Wait until the box disappears on its own to go back to Zotero.

Now, back in Zotero, your items should appear with PDFs of the resource. Double click the item to start reading. If you get any resources without PDF's you will have to upload them. The next slide shows you how to do so.
You have an item's record in your application, but no PDF? Let's get the PDF uploaded to it so that you can read it.

params.text: Here's the "Download PDF" button! Let's click on it and save the PDF to our computer. Next, we're back in the Zotero app.

metadata.title: Let's go back to ProQuest and find the "Download PDF" button and click it.

params.text: Click on the item to highlight it in blue and have the column on the right appear.
Next we're going to scroll down the column until we reach a section titled "Attachments"
Click on the "+"
Click on "File"

metadata.title: Okay, we're back in Zotero. Click on the item to highlight it in blue and on the right side you should have a column appear. Find the "Attachments" section in the column and select "+". Next, select "File".
Select the downloaded PDF and hit "Open"
Double click on the resource and congradulations!! Now you can start reading, highlightling and mapping your research in Zotero!

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Basics - Downloading the app and connector
feedback.subtitle: What else would you like to explore?

alternatives.text: Bibliography (list of references at the end of your paper)

branchingQuestion.question: Do you want to explore citation (single/few, in-text citations) or a bibliography (list of references at the end of your paper) generation?

metadata.title: Select a resource. You have a resources selected once it is highlighted in blue
Select a resource. You have a resource selected once it is highlighted in blue.
Right click on your mouse to get this dialog pop-up.
Go to the bottom of the pop-up and select "Create Bibliography from Item".

params.text: Select your citation style. In this example, I am using American Psychological Association (APA) 7th edition. APA style is the standard for all undergraduate classes at NCU. For graduate classes you may also be asked to use Society of Biblical Literature (SBL) which is also avaliable in the Zotero citation menu.
Ensure your lanuage is set correctly
Ensure your output is set to "Citations"
Select your output method. We reccommend copying to your clipboard so you can paste this citation into your paper.
Click "Okay" to move forward

metadata.title: Next, you should get this dialog box pop-up. Select the citation style you are using, English as your language, make sure the output mode is "Citations", and select your output mode. We reccommend "Copy to Clipboard". Click the "Okay" button.

params.text: (Mockler, 1991)

metadata.title: Great! Now, you are ready to paste or use your citation. To paste, select the keys "control" + "F" on your keyboard to paste. (On a MAC this will be: "cmd" + "F"). It should appear similar to:

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Citations and Bibliography - Citations
feedback.subtitle: What else would you like to explore?

metadata.title: Next, select the resources you want to include in your bibliography. To select multiple resources, hold your "control" key on your keyboard and select the resources you want to include. (On a MAC this with be the "cmd" key).
Next, right click using your mouse. This should open up a dialog box like the one below.
In this new dialog box, navigate to the bottom and click on "Create Bibliography from Items".

params.text: Select your citation style. In this example, I am using American Psychological Association (APA) 7th edition. APA style is the standard for all undergraduate classes at NCU. For graduate classes you may also be asked to use Society of Biblical Literature (SBL) which is also avaliable in the Zotero citation menu.
Ensure your lanuage is set correctly
Ensure your output is set to "Bibliography"
Select your output method. We reccommend copying to your clipboard so you can paste this citation into your paper.
Click "Okay" to move forward.

metadata.title: Next, you should get this dialog box pop-up. Select the citation style you are using, English as your language, make sure the output mode is "Bibliography", and select your output mode. We reccommend "Copy to Clipboard". Click the "Okay" button.

params.text:
Begun, J. W., Kaissi, A. A., & Sweetland, D. L. (2005). An Exploratory Study of Healthcare Strategic Planning in Two Metropolitan Areas/PRACTITIONER APPLICATION. Journal of Healthcare Management, 50(4), 264–274; discussion 274-5.

Cohen, M. P. (2006). Public sector strategic planning: Is it really planning or is it really strategy or is it neither or both? [D.M., University of Maryland University College]. In ProQuest Dissertations and Theses. https://www.proquest.com/pq1business/docview/304913942/abstract/177572EA1AD146FBPQ/16

Dhlamini, J. (2024). Understanding strategic planning: An assessment of the definitions, planning process and competitive advantage. Strategy & Leadership, 52(5/6), 64–81. https://doi.org/10.1108/SL-09-2024-0083

Dhlamini, J. (2025). Exploring the rise and fall of strategic planning: Is strategic planning still a useful management practice in the 21st century? Strategy & Leadership, 53(4), 457. https://doi.org/10.1108/SL-11-2024-0131

Haleem, F., Jehangir, M., & Ullah, Z. (2019). Strategic Planning and SMEs Performance: A Developing Country’s Perspective. Journal of Business & Economics, 11(2), 33–49.

Hughes, P., & Hodgkinson, I. (2021). Knowledge management activities and strategic planning capability development. European Business Review, 33(2), 238–254. https://doi.org/10.1108/EBR-03-2019-0034

Hunitie, M. (2018). Impact of strategic leadership on strategic competitive advantage through strategic thinking and strategic planning: A bi-meditational research. Verslas : Teorija Ir Praktika, 19, 322–330. https://doi.org/10.3846/btp.2018.32

Lenihan, D. P. (2001). Strategic planning in public health [Ph.D., University of Illinois at Chicago]. In ProQuest Dissertations and Theses. https://www.proquest.com/pq1business/docview/304765599/abstract/177572EA1AD146FBPQ/4

Lipitakis, A., & Lipitakis, E. A. (2017). On e-business strategy planning and performance evaluation: An adaptive algorithmic managerial approach. International Journal of Business and Economic Development (IJBED), 5(2). https://www.proquest.com/pq1business/docview/1934153776/abstract/177572EA1AD146FBPQ/7

Mansor, M., & Tayib, M. (2012). Strategic Planning in Public Organization: The Case of a Tax Administration in a Developing Country. Business Management Dynamics, 1(8), 20–33.

Mert, G., Akkaya, B., Palazzo, M., Micozzi, A., Ferri, A., & Notari, F. (2025). Investigating the relationships among organizational policy, strategic planning performance, and business ethics in manufacturing and service industries. International Entrepreneurship and Management Journal, 21(1), 68. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11365-025-01077-6

Mockler, R. J. (1991). A Catalog of Commercially Available Software for Strategic Planning. Planning Review, 19(3), 28. https://doi.org/10.1108/eb054326

Nataraja, S. (2014). Perceptions of business school leaders and faculty members on strategic planning implications at a selected business school [Ed.D., University of South Dakota]. In ProQuest Dissertations and Theses. https://www.proquest.com/pq1business/docview/1619368742/abstract/177572EA1AD146FBPQ/2

Oschman, J. J. (2017). The Role of Strategic Planning in Implementing a Total Quality Management Framework: An Empirical View. The Quality Management Journal, 24(2), 41-53,4-5.

Petkovic, J., Jasinskas, E., & Jeseviciuté-Ufartiené, L. (2016). Significance of Strategic Planning for Results of Sport Organization. E+M Ekonomie a Management, 19(4), 56–72. https://doi.org/10.15240/tul/001/2016-4-005

Rollinson, R. (2011). Should strategy professionals be certified? Strategy & Leadership, 39(1), 39–43. https://doi.org/10.1108/10878571111095411

Stewart, K. S. (2004). The relationship between strategic planning and growth in small businesses [D.B.A., Nova Southeastern University]. In ProQuest Dissertations and Theses. https://www.proquest.com/pq1business/docview/305044547/abstract/177572EA1AD146FBPQ/19

Täks, V., & Vadi, M. (2019). Who and how do participate strategic planning? IDEAS Working Paper Series from RePEc. https://www.proquest.com/pq1business/docview/2215728083/177572EA1AD146FBPQ/6

Toles, K. (2020). Sustainability of Small Businesses Through Strategic Planning and Management [D.B.A., Capella University]. In ProQuest Dissertations and Theses. https://www.proquest.com/pq1business/docview/2478009016/abstract/177572EA1AD146FBPQ/15

Zuckerman, A. M. (2006). Advancing the State of the Art in Healthcare Strategic Planning. Frontiers of Health Services Management, 23(2), 3–15.

metadata.title: Great! Now, you are ready to paste or use your citation. To paste, select the keys "control" + "F" on your keyboard to paste. (On a MAC this will be: "cmd" + "F"). It should appear similar to:

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Citations and Bibliography - Bibliography
feedback.subtitle: What else would you like to explore?

metadata.title: Select a resource. You have a resources selected once it is highlighted in blue

Next, select the resources you want to include in your export to Academic Writer. To select multiple resources, hold your "control" key on your keyboard and select the resources you want to include. (On a MAC this with be the "cmd" key).
Next, right click using your mouse. This should open up a dialog box like the one below.

params.text: You want your format to be Research Information Services (RIS). This is the most data rich format to upload into Academic Writer.
You want to export notes here because otherwise the system will try to upload PDFs into Academic Writer which is not supported.
Next, you want to ensure that your Character Encoding is UTF-8. This perserves any names or titles that may have diacritics in them such as accents or special characters.
Click "Okay" to start the download process.

metadata.title: A smaller pop-up should open. Select "RIS" format, "Export Notes", and you want your character encoding to be UTF-8. Select "Okay" to move forward.

params.text: Click here!

metadata.title: Go to academicwriter.apa.org and click on "Add References". This will prompt you to login.

params.text: Click here on the "Import" tab.

metadata.title: Once you are logged in and in your reference center, you should see the screen below. Click on the "Import" tab.

params.text: Click here!

metadata.title: Next, go to "Choose RIS file Origin" and select the "Select File Origin" drop down list.

params.text: Select "Zotero" from the list. You may have to scroll to the bottom.

metadata.title: Select "Zotero" from the drop down list and go to the "Upload RIS File" section. Click on the box: "Choose File"

params.text: Select the RIS file we downloaded earlier from Zotero
Click, "Open"

metadata.title: In the pop-up, selected the RIS file you downloaded from Zotero and click "Open".

params.text: If any required information is missing, the reference will be marked as Verification Needed (!). If a reference is missing information, select Edit and add any information that is missing. It is important to always look through all of your reference whether they are marked or not to verify that they are correct. The software is fast and helpful, but not perfect.

metadata.title: Congradulations! Your references have been imported and are ready to be used. *Notice that my second reference have an exclaimation point* This means that it did not import correctly.  If any required APA information is missing, it will have "!".

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero and Academic Writer
feedback.subtitle: What would you like to explore next?

params.text: Notice this says "Zotero"
Notice my file name is here
Click submit!

metadata.title: Once the file has been uploaded, you should be back in Academic Writer and your screen should reflect the one below. Select the green "Submit" button to start the import process.

params.text: Click here to verify all information transferred correctly

metadata.title: And just like that, you have mass imported your citations into Academic Writer. Click the green "View Imported Records" button to the right.

params.text: I am saving this to my downloads folder.
Name your file here.
Click "Save"

metadata.title: Select where the download will save to. You want this to be somewhere you will be able to find it again, name it and, select "Save".

params.text: Click here!

metadata.title: In this new dialog box, navigate to the bottom and click on "Export Items".

params.text: Click here!

metadata.title: Next, Go to the "Upload RIS File" section and select "Choose File"

branchingQuestion.question: Which Research Tools would you like to explore?

params.text: Double click on the resource you want to read
If nothing happens, make sure there is a PDF icon here. If not, like this Dhlamini source, you will have to attach one. Check out Zotero Troubleshooting for more help on this.

metadata.title: Start in your Zotero Library and double click the resource you want you read. (If nothing opens, make sure it has a PDF icon)

params.text: Click this highlight tool!

metadata.title: Now, you are in Reading Mode. Go to the top center bar for reading tools and click the highlight tool. Now, any text you select in the article will be highlighted!

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Research Tools - Highlighting
feedback.subtitle: What else would you like to explore?

params.text: Click this underline tool!

metadata.title: Now, you are in Reading Mode. Go to the top center bar for reading tools and click the underline tool. Now, any text you select in the article will be underlined!

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Research Tools - Underlining

metadata.title: Start in your Zotero Library and double click the resource you want you read. (If nothing opens, make sure it has a PDF icon)

params.text: Click this annotate tool!

metadata.title: Now, you are in Reading Mode. Go to the top center bar for reading tools and click the annotate tool. Now, anywhere you click on the article will have a stickynote where you can add annotations!

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Research Tools - Annotations
feedback.subtitle: What else would you like to explore?

params.text: Click this Text tool!

metadata.title: Now, you are in Reading Mode. Go to the top center bar for reading tools and click the Text tool. Now, anywhere you click on the article will have a cursor where you can type text!

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Research Tools - Text-to-Text
feedback.subtitle: What else would you like to explore?

params.text: Click this Box tool!

metadata.title: Now, you are in Reading Mode. Go to the top center bar for reading tools and click the Box tool. Now, anywhere you click on the article will have a Box highlighting a segment of the article!

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Research Tools - Box
feedback.subtitle: What else would you like to explore?

params.text: Click this Draw tool!

metadata.title: Now, you are in Reading Mode. Go to the top center bar for reading tools and click the Draw tool. Now, anywhere you click on the article will be able to draw or make hand-written notes! This is especially helpful if you ever work on an ipad.

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Research Tools - Draw
feedback.subtitle: What else would you like to explore?

params.text: Click this Search tool!

metadata.title: Now, you are in Reading Mode. Go to the top center bar for reading tools and click the Search tool. Now, type in any keywords you are looking for. This is especially helpful if you're looking to do research quickly or are looking for specific quotes..

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Research Tools - Draw
feedback.subtitle: What else would you like to explore?

alternatives.text: Item Record (this is to tell your resources apart from one another)
Article (this is to make notes as you are reading the research)

branchingQuestion.question: There are two ways to add tags and notes. Do you want to add tags/notes to the item record or the article as you read it?

metadata.title: There are two ways to add tags and notes. Do you want to add tags/notes to the item record or the article as you read it?

branchingQuestion.question: Would you like to explore notes or tags?
Would you like to explore notes/comments or tags?

params.text: Click your reference
This column should appear after you click on your resource

metadata.title: Start in your Zotero Library and Select the item to which you would like to add notes. This will open a column to the right of the screen.

params.text: This is the notes section
Click here!

metadata.title: Scroll down the column until you reach the "Notes" Section. Click the "+" to the right.

params.text: You can also click here to create a new note or to create a "Standalone Note" that is not connected to any one resource.
Type your notes in here. There are text formatting options, highlighting, linking, citation and search funtions at the top of the page as well.

metadata.title: Type your notes in the new note you created! You can also create "Standalone Notes" that are not attached to any item but are saved among your rerferences.

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Research Tools - Item Record: Notes
feedback.subtitle: Where do you want to explore next?

params.text: Click your reference
This column should appear after you click on your resource

metadata.title: Start in your Zotero Library and Select the item to which you would like to add notes. This will open a column to the right of the screen.

params.text: This is the tags section
Click here!

metadata.title: Scroll down the column until you reach the "Tags" Section. Click the "+" to the right.

params.text: Type in your new tag/lable

metadata.title: A dialog box will open where you can add your new tag/lable!

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Research Tools Item Record: Tags
feedback.subtitle: Where would you like to explore next?

params.text: This item is in reading mode
In order to add notes/comments or tags, the item must have an edit from one of these tools

metadata.title: Notes/Comments can be added to text once the item is in reading mode and the text has an edit. These edits can be any of the features on the top bar such as highlights, underlines, annotations, text-in-text, boxes or the draw tool.

params.text: Double click on the resource you want to read
If nothing happens, make sure there is a PDF icon here. If not, like this Dhlamini source, you will have to attach one. Check out Zotero Troubleshooting for more help on this.

metadata.title: Start in your Zotero Library and double click the resource you want you read. (If nothing opens, make sure it has a PDF icon)

params.text: Here is my edit I made with the highlight tool
Click on your edit in the column and a "add comment" box will appear.
Type your comment/note here, and voila! You now have notes within your research.

metadata.title: Once the resource has an edit, such as this highlight, a column on the left side will open. This column allows you to explore all of your edits. It also allows you to add notes/comments to your edit by giving you a comment box under the edit.

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Research Tools Article: Notes/Comments
feedback.subtitle: Where would you like to explore next?

params.text: Double click on the resource you want to read
If nothing happens, make sure there is a PDF icon here. If not, like this Dhlamini source, you will have to attach one. Check out Zotero Troubleshooting for more help on this.

metadata.title: Start in your Zotero Library and double click the resource you want you read. (If nothing opens, make sure it has a PDF icon)

params.text: This item is in reading mode
In order to add notes/comments or tags, the item must have an edit from one of these tools

metadata.title: Tags can be added to text once the item is in reading mode and the text has an edit. These edits can be any of the features on the top bar such as highlights, underlines, annotations, text-in-text, boxes or the draw tool.

params.text: Here is my edit I made with the highlight tool
Click on your edit in the column and a "Add tags" box will appear.
Type your tag here, and voila! You now have tags to organize your research.

metadata.title: Once the resource has an edit, such as this highlight, a column on the left side will open. This column allows you to explore all of your edits. It also allows you to add tags to your edit by giving you "Add tags" button under the edit.

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Research Tools Article: Tags
feedback.subtitle: Where would you like to explore next?

feedback.subtitle: What else would you like to explore?

params.text: This is where you can change reading settings such as how the document scrolls, how it is split, and the reading theme. Now your Zotero's appearance has been customized!

metadata.title: In the drop down, click whichever setting you would like to change such as how the document scrolls, how it is split, and the reading theme.

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Research Tools - Text Settings
feedback.subtitle: What else would you like to explore?

params.text: Click this listening tool!

metadata.title: Now, you are in Reading Mode. Go to the top left bar for reading tools and click the listening tool. Now, you should have a drop down menu.

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Research Tools - Annotations
feedback.subtitle: What else would you like to explore?

params.text: Select the voice you like best
By hitting the play button here you can listen to a preview of each voice.
Once you have found a voice you like hit the "save" button at the bottom

metadata.title: In the pop-up, click whichever voice you want to read to you and select "Save".

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Research Tools - Text Settings
feedback.subtitle: What else would you like to explore?

params.text: Here is your audio menu
Here is where you can change the voice, language and speed.
Here, you can change what text is being read

metadata.title: Now that you have selected a voice, you have a play menu. It has a few settings you can explore such as changing the voice you selected, its language or what segement of text it is reading aloud.

feedback.title: YAY! You've explored Zotero Research Tools - Read Aloud
feedback.subtitle: What else would you like to explore?

startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: Learn how to use Zotero citation manager to help you do research quicker, more effectively and helps you stay organized as you read.

l10n.disableProceedButtonText: Require to complete the current module
